lines.translation: Tu dois être épuisé, n'est-ce pas ?
Je vais bien. Papa, Maman, je vous présente mon camarade de classe, Li You.
Li You, tu parles vraiment bien chinois.
Merci. C'est parce que Wang Peng m'a bien appris.
Pas du tout, c'est parce que tu es intelligent.
Hé, vous êtes tous les deux intelligents.
Xiao Peng, tu as l'air d'avoir maigri. Étais-tu trop occupé par ton petit boulot pour manger ?
Je n'ai pas maigri. Je fais souvent de l'exercice, mon corps est bien plus fort qu'avant.
Elle va très bien, son anglais s'est beaucoup amélioré.
Allons-y. On pourra discuter tranquillement une fois dans la voiture. Papi et Mamie nous attendent au restaurant de canard laqué !

language_notes.label: a
language_notes.explanation_en: Les jeunes s'adressent souvent à des personnes qui ne sont pas de leur famille en utilisant 叔叔 [zh:shūshu], qui signifie oncle, ou 阿姨 [zh:āyí], qui signifie tante, si la personne a à peu près le même âge que leurs parents. Ces formes de politesse peuvent même être utilisées avec des inconnus. [See Grammar 5 for additional terms for kin.]

grammar_sections.title: 还 [zh:hái] + adjectif positif
grammar_sections.explanation_en: 还 [zh:hái] peut être utilisé avant un adjectif positif pour indiquer que quelque chose est acceptable, mais pas exceptionnel.

examples.hanzi:
A: 你对那家旅馆的印象怎么样?
B: 还好。
examples.pinyin:
A: Nǐ duì nà jiā lǚguǎn de yìnxiàng zěnmeyàng?
B: Hái hǎo.
examples.translation:
A : Quelle a été votre impression de cet hôtel ?
B : C'est pas mal.

examples.hanzi: 这个厨房还可以, 挺干净的。
examples.pinyin: Zhè ge chúfáng hái kěyǐ, tǐng gānjìng de.
examples.translation: Cette cuisine est pas mal. Elle est assez propre.

examples.hanzi: 那套公寓还行, 带家具。
examples.pinyin: Nà tào gōngyù hái xíng, dài jiājù.
examples.translation: Cet appartement n'est pas trop mal. Il est meublé.

examples.hanzi: 那个饭馆的红烧牛肉和家常豆腐还不错。
examples.pinyin: Nà ge fànguǎn de hóngshāo niúròu hé jiācháng dòufu hái búcuò.
examples.translation: Le bœuf braisé à la sauce soja et le tofu maison de ce restaurant sont plutôt bons.

grammar_sections.title: Termes de parenté
grammar_sections.explanation_en: Le système des termes de parenté en chinois est assez complexe, notamment parce que les Chinois distinguent les parents paternels et maternels, les frères et sœurs aînés et cadets, et ainsi de suite. Les tableaux suivants présentent les termes de parenté chinois.

examples.hanzi: 妈妈的姐姐我们叫阿姨。
examples.pinyin: māma de jiějie wǒmen jiào āyí.
examples.translation: On appelle la sœur aînée de la mère 'ayi'.

usage_notes: Dans les tableaux, le guillemet double indique un usage du nord, l'astérisque un usage du sud, et le 'f' une forme d'adresse plus formelle.

Les jeunes s'adressent souvent à des personnes qui ne font pas partie de leur famille en les appelant 叔叔 [zh:shūshu] (oncle) ou 阿姨 [zh:āyí] (tante) si ces personnes ont à peu près le même âge que leurs parents. Ces formes d'adresse respectueuses peuvent être utilisées même avec des inconnus.

global_config.visual_style: Cinematic, realistic, warm color palette, shallow depth of field to focus on characters.
global_config.setting_summary_cn: 留学生王朋和他的同学李友一起抵达北京首都机场。王朋的父母前来接机，一家人温馨团聚，并初次见到了李友。
global_config.setting_summary_en: Student Wang Peng and his classmate Li You arrive at Beijing Capital Airport. Wang Peng's parents are there to pick them up, leading to a warm family reunion and their first meeting with Li You.

character_definitions.name: 王朋
character_definitions.appearance_description_en: A Chinese male student in his early 20s, dressed in a casual hoodie and jeans. He looks happy but a bit tired from the long flight. He is pushing a luggage cart with a large suitcase and a backpack.

character_definitions.name: 李友
character_definitions.appearance_description_en: A non-Chinese female student in her early 20s (e.g., American), with a friendly and curious expression. Dressed comfortably for travel, standing next to Wang Peng with her own carry-on luggage.

character_definitions.name: 爸爸
character_definitions.appearance_description_en: A Chinese man in his 50s, with glasses. He has a kind, proud face and is dressed in a smart-casual jacket. He looks very excited to see his son.

character_definitions.name: 妈妈
character_definitions.appearance_description_en: A Chinese woman in her 50s, with a gentle and caring face, dressed elegantly. Her expression is a mix of joy and concern for her son.

scenes.scene_goal: 表现王朋一家在机场重逢的激动与关切。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 北京首都机场国际到达大厅，人潮涌动。王朋的爸爸在人群中踮起脚尖张望，突然眼睛一亮，激动地向刚走出闸口的儿子挥手大喊。王朋也看到了父母，开心地回应，快步推着行李车走上前。妈妈立刻迎上去，心疼地抚摸儿子的手臂。
scenes.setting_en: Beijing Capital Airport, international arrivals hall. Crowds of people waiting.
scenes.camera_movement: The camera pans across the waiting crowd, then a quick zoom to Dad's excited face. It cuts to Wang Peng's perspective, then tracks him as he walks towards his parents.
scenes.shot_type: Wide shot, followed by medium close-ups and a tracking shot.
scenes.video_prompt_en: A bustling airport arrivals hall. A middle-aged Chinese man (Dad) spots his son, his face lighting up. He waves and calls out 'Xiao Peng!'. Cut to a young man (Wang Peng) who smiles brightly and calls back 'Dad, Mom!', pushing his luggage cart towards them. As they meet, the mother steps forward, her face full of concern, and gently touches his arm.

hanzi: 还好。爸，妈，我给你们介绍一下，这是我的同学李友。
pinyin: hái hǎo bà mā wǒ gěi nǐmen jièshào yíxià zhè shì wǒ de tóngxué Lǐ Yǒu
translation: I'm okay. Dad, Mom, let me introduce you, this is my classmate Li You.

scenes.scene_goal: 王朋向父母介绍同学李友，李友礼貌地问好。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 王朋微笑着摇摇头，示意自己不累。然后他自然地侧过身，把一直安静地站在旁边的李友介绍给父母。李友向前一步，微笑着向王朋的父母鞠躬问好。
scenes.setting_en: Beijing Capital Airport, in front of the arrivals gate.
scenes.camera_movement: The camera is stable, then slightly pans from Wang Peng to Li You as he gestures.
scenes.video_prompt_en: Wang Peng smiles reassuringly at his mother. He then turns and gestures towards his friend, Li You, who has been standing by quietly. As he speaks, the camera focus shifts to Li You, who steps forward, gives a small, polite bow, and greets the parents with a friendly smile.

voice_over.speaker: 王朋
voice_over.hanzi: 还好。爸，妈，我给你们介绍一下，这是我的同学李友。
voice_over.pinyin: hái hǎo bà mā wǒ gěi nǐmen jièshào yíxià zhè shì wǒ de tóngxué Lǐ Yǒu
voice_over.translation: I'm okay. Dad, Mom, let me introduce you, this is my classmate Li You.

hanzi: 李友，你的中文说得真好。
pinyin: huān yíng nǐ lái Běi jīng
Lǐ Yǒu nǐ de Zhōng wén shuō de zhēn hǎo
translation: Li You, you speak Chinese really well.

scenes.scene_goal: 王朋的父母热情地欢迎李友，并称赞她的中文水平。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 听到李友流利的问候，王朋的爸爸立刻和蔼地笑着欢迎她。妈妈也紧接着走上前，亲切地看着李友，用惊喜和赞许的语气夸奖她的中文。李友听了有点不好意思地笑了。
scenes.setting_en: Beijing Capital Airport, the family group is now interacting.
scenes.camera_movement: Cut from a shot of Dad smiling to a close-up on Mom as she speaks to Li You.
scenes.shot_type: Medium close-ups on the parents and Li You.
scenes.video_prompt_en: The father smiles warmly at Li You and says 'Welcome to Beijing'. The camera then cuts to the mother, who looks at Li You with an impressed and kind expression. She compliments Li You on her Chinese. Li You reacts with a pleased and slightly shy smile.

voice_over.speaker: 妈妈
voice_over.hanzi: 李友，你的中文说得真好。
voice_over.pinyin: Lǐ Yǒu nǐ de Zhōng wén shuō de zhēn hǎo
voice_over.translation: Li You, you speak Chinese really well.

hanzi: 小朋，你好像瘦了点儿。是不是打工太忙，没有时间吃饭？
我没瘦。我常常运动，身体比以前棒多了。
pinyin: Xiǎo Péng nǐ hǎoxiàng shòu le diǎnr shì bu shì dǎ gōng tài máng méiyǒu shíjiān chī fàn
wǒ méi shòu wǒ chángcháng yùndòng shēntǐ bǐ yǐqián bàng duō le
translation: Xiao Peng, you seem to have lost some weight. Were you too busy with your part-time job to eat?
I haven't lost weight. I exercise often, my body is much stronger than before.

scenes.scene_goal: 表现母亲对儿子的关爱，以及儿子健康自信的形象。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 妈妈心疼地看着王朋，觉得他瘦了，关切地询问。王朋笑着回应自己没有瘦，还俏皮地弯起胳膊展示肌肉，证明自己身体很棒，让妈妈放心。
scenes.setting_en: Beijing Capital International Airport, arrivals hall. Crowds of people are moving around. The family is standing in a relatively clear spot.
scenes.camera_movement: Starts as a medium shot on the family, then slowly pushes in to a close-up on the mother's concerned face as she speaks, then cuts to Wang Peng's reaction.
scenes.video_prompt_en: A medium shot of a family reunion at a busy airport. A middle-aged Chinese mother looks at her son (early 20s) with a concerned expression, touching his arm. She asks if he has lost weight. The son smiles, shakes his head, and playfully flexes his bicep to reassure her he is strong and healthy. Cinematic, warm lighting.

voice_over.speaker: 妈妈
voice_over.hanzi: 小朋，你好像瘦了点儿。是不是打工太忙，没有时间吃饭？
voice_over.pinyin: Xiǎo Péng nǐ hǎoxiàng shòu le diǎnr shì bu shì dǎ gōng tài máng méiyǒu shíjiān chī fàn
voice_over.translation: Xiao Peng, you seem to have lost some weight. Were you too busy with your part-time job to eat?

hanzi: 她很好，英文水平提高了很多。
pinyin: tā hěn hǎo yīngwén shuǐpíng tígāo le hěn duō
translation: She's doing great, her English has improved a lot.

scenes.scene_goal: 通过对话自然地引出另一个角色“小红”的情况。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 爸爸看到母子俩的互动，笑着转换了话题，向王朋询问小红的情况。王朋自豪地回答说小红很好，英语进步很大。李友在一旁听着，露出感兴趣的表情。
scenes.setting_en: Beijing Capital International Airport, arrivals hall. The family continues their conversation.
scenes.camera_movement: Pan slightly from Wang Peng to his father as the father asks the question. Then cut back to a medium shot of Wang Peng as he answers, with Li You visible in the background.
scenes.video_prompt_en: A medium shot in a busy airport. A Chinese father in his 50s smiles and asks his son about 'Xiao Hong'. The son replies with a proud smile, explaining that her English has improved a lot. An American girl, Li You, listens with interest in the background.

hanzi: 走吧，我们上车以后，再慢慢儿地聊吧。爷爷、奶奶在烤鸭店等我们呢！
pinyin: zǒu ba wǒmen shàng chē yǐhòu zài mànmānr de liáo ba yéye nǎinai zài kǎoyādiàn děng wǒmen ne
translation: Let's go. We can chat slowly after we get in the car. Grandpa and Grandma are waiting for us at the roast duck restaurant!

scenes.scene_goal: 推动剧情发展，引出下一个场景（烤鸭店），并展现李友对中国美食的期待。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 爸爸看了看周围的人流，拍了拍王朋的肩膀，提议大家先上车，并宣布了一个好消息：爷爷奶奶已经在烤鸭店等着他们了。镜头快速切换到李友，她听到“烤鸭店”三个字，眼睛一亮，露出惊喜又好奇的表情。
scenes.setting_en: Beijing Capital International Airport, arrivals hall, near the exit.
scenes.camera_movement: The camera follows the father as he gestures towards the exit. Then a whip pan or quick cut to a close-up on Li You's face to capture her excited reaction.
scenes.video_prompt_en: A Chinese father gestures towards the airport exit, suggesting they leave and mentions that grandpa and grandma are waiting at a roast duck restaurant. The family starts to move. Quick cut to a close-up of Li You, an American girl, her eyes widening with excitement and curiosity as she repeats 'Roast duck restaurant?'.

voice_over.speaker: 爸爸
voice_over.hanzi: 走吧，我们上车以后，再慢慢儿地聊吧。爷爷、奶奶在烤鸭店等我们呢！
voice_over.pinyin: zǒu ba wǒmen shàng chē yǐhòu zài mànmānr de liáo ba yéye nǎinai zài kǎoyādiàn děng wǒmen ne
voice_over.translation: Let's go. We can chat slowly after we get in the car. Grandpa and Grandma are waiting for us at the roast duck restaurant!

global_config.teaching_style: A friendly and structured approach, breaking down dialogue line by line with clear explanations of vocabulary and cultural context. Focuses on building foundational understanding before moving to complex grammar.
global_config.visual_style: Clean, modern educational cards with animated text and character highlights.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand the setting of the dialogue: Beijing Capital Airport.

narration.subtitle_en: Our lesson begins with a scene-setting description. The text says [zh:在北京首都机场], which means 'At the Beijing Capital Airport'. Let's break this down: [zh:北京] is Beijing, the capital of China. [zh:首都] means 'capital city', and [zh:机场] means 'airport'. So, the family reunion is happening right at the airport.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The capital city of China.
The primary city of a country, serving as the seat of government.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: Composed of [zh:首] (shǒu), meaning 'head' or 'first', and [zh:都] (dū), meaning 'metropolis' or 'capital'. Together, they literally mean 'head city'.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A place where aircraft regularly take off and land.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: Made of [zh:机] (jī), short for [zh:飞机] (fēijī) 'airplane', and [zh:场] (chǎng), meaning 'field' or 'large open space'. So, an 'airplane field'.

segments.visual_notes: Show an animation of an airport arrivals hall. Highlight the characters 北京, 首都, and 机场 as they are explained.

segments.teaching_goal: To recognize a simple, direct greeting using a person's name.

narration.subtitle_en: The father sees his son and calls out his name, [zh:小朋]. This is a common and affectionate way to get someone's attention. 'Xiao' is often used as a prefix for the names of younger people, making it sound friendly.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a speech bubble from the father character.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the most common terms for 'Dad' and 'Mom'.

narration.subtitle_en: Wang Peng responds to his father by calling out to both of his parents: [zh:爸，妈]. [zh:爸] is the informal and most common word for 'Dad', and [zh:妈] is for 'Mom'. These are the first words most Chinese children learn for their parents.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Informal term for father.
Informal term for mother.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a speech bubble from Wang Peng. Highlight the characters 爸 and 妈.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to ask if someone is exhausted using '累坏了' and the suggestion particle '吧'.

narration.subtitle_en: The mother shows her concern after a long flight. She asks, [zh:累坏了吧]. Let's break this down. [zh:累] means 'tired'. Adding [zh:坏] after it acts as a complement, intensifying the meaning to 'exhausted' or 'terribly tired'. The final particle [zh:吧] softens the question, turning it into a gentle assumption, like 'You must be exhausted, right?'.

on_screen_text.notes: Structure: Adjective + 坏了 (huài le) = extremely [Adjective]

highlight_words.explanation_en: Feeling in need of rest or sleep.
When used as a complement after an adjective, it indicates an extreme degree.

segments.visual_notes: Animate the sentence construction: 累 + 坏 + 了 + 吧. Show an icon for 'tired' and an intensifier icon for '坏'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to say 'I'm okay' and introduce a classmate.

narration.subtitle_en: Wang Peng replies that he's fine and introduces his friend. He starts with [zh:还好], which means 'I'm okay' or 'not bad'. Then he uses the key phrase for introductions: [zh:我给你们介绍一下], 'Let me introduce to you all'. Finally, he says [zh:这是我的同学李友], 'This is my classmate, Li You'. The word [zh:同学] means 'classmate'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 还好。爸，妈，我给你们介绍一下，这是我的同学李友。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: hái hǎo. bà, mā, wǒ gěi nǐmen jièshào yíxià, zhè shì wǒ de tóngxué Lǐ Yǒu.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I'm okay. Dad, Mom, let me introduce you, this is my classmate Li You.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Indicates something is acceptable but not outstanding.
To present someone or something to another.
Used after a verb to indicate a brief action.
A fellow student in a class.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: Composed of [zh:同] (tóng) 'same' and [zh:学] (xué) 'to study'. Literally, 'same study' person.

segments.visual_notes: Highlight the key phrase '我给你们介绍一下'. Show icons for Wang Peng, his parents, and Li You to visualize the introduction.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to respectfully greet a friend's parents using '叔叔' and '阿姨'.

narration.subtitle_en: Li You greets Wang Peng's parents very politely. She says, [zh:叔叔，阿姨，你们好]. In Chinese culture, it's common to address people of your parents' generation as [zh:叔叔] for men, meaning 'uncle', and [zh:阿姨] for women, meaning 'auntie', even if they are not related to you. This is a sign of respect and friendliness.

on_screen_text.notes: Use '叔叔' (shūshu) and '阿姨' (āyí) to respectfully address non-relatives of your parents' age.

highlight_words.translation: uncle (also a respectful term for an unrelated man of one's father's age)
highlight_words.explanation_en: A polite form of address for a man from a younger person.

highlight_words.translation: aunt (also a respectful term for an unrelated woman of one's mother's age)
highlight_words.explanation_en: A polite form of address for a woman from a younger person.

segments.visual_notes: Show Li You bowing slightly as she speaks. Highlight '叔叔' and '阿姨' with a note about their cultural usage.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to welcome someone using '欢迎'.

narration.subtitle_en: The father responds warmly to Li You. He says, [zh:欢迎你来北京]. The key word here is [zh:欢迎], which means 'welcome'. The full sentence means 'Welcome to Beijing'. This is a very common and hospitable phrase you will hear often when arriving in a new place in China.

highlight_words.explanation_en: To greet someone in a polite or friendly way when they arrive.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: Made of [zh:欢] (huān) 'joyous' and [zh:迎] (yíng) 'to receive' or 'to greet'. Together, it means to 'joyously receive' someone.

segments.visual_notes: Animate the word '欢迎' with sparkles or a welcoming gesture.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the structure for complimenting an action: Verb + 得 + Adverb + Adjective.

narration.subtitle_en: The mother compliments Li You on her language skills. She says, [zh:你的中文说得真好], 'Your Chinese is spoken really well.' Let's focus on the structure [zh:说得真好]. [zh:说] is the verb 'to speak'. It's followed by the particle [zh:得], which connects the verb to a description of how well the action is done. [zh:真好] means 'really good'. This 'verb-得-description' pattern is essential for giving compliments.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 李友，你的中文说得真好。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Lǐ Yǒu, nǐ de Zhōngwén shuō de zhēn hǎo.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Li You, you speak Chinese really well.
on_screen_text.notes: Structure for compliments: Verb + 得 (de) + Description

highlight_words.explanation_en: The language of China.
A phrase describing the quality of speaking.
An adverb used to emphasize an adjective or verb.

segments.visual_notes: Animate the structure: 说 (verb) + 得 + 真好 (description).

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to modestly accept a compliment and give credit to someone else.

narration.subtitle_en: Li You responds to the compliment with modesty, a valued trait in Chinese culture. She first says [zh:谢谢], 'Thank you'. Then, she gives credit to her friend: [zh:是因为王朋教得好], 'It's because Wang Peng taught well'. The phrase [zh:是因为] emphasizes the reason. Notice the same pattern again: [zh:教得好], 'teaches well'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 谢谢。是因为王朋教得好。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: xièxie. shì yīnwèi Wáng Péng jiāo de hǎo.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Thanks. It's because Wang Peng taught me well.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A polite expression of gratitude.
Used to introduce a reason or cause.
To impart knowledge or skill.

segments.visual_notes: Show Li You smiling and gesturing towards Wang Peng.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the phrase '哪里' as a polite way to deflect a compliment.

narration.subtitle_en: Wang Peng also shows modesty. He says, [zh:哪里], which literally means 'where?' but is used here as a humble way to say 'Not at all' or 'You're flattering me' when receiving a compliment. He then returns the compliment to Li You, saying [zh:是因为你聪明], 'It's because you're smart'. The word for 'smart' is [zh:聪明].

on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Not at all, it's because you're smart.
on_screen_text.notes: 哪里 (nǎli) is a common, polite way to deflect a compliment.

highlight_words.translation: not at all (in response to a compliment)
highlight_words.explanation_en: A polite expression to humbly deny a compliment, literally meaning 'where'.
Having or showing a quick-witted intelligence.

segments.visual_notes: Highlight '哪里' and add a cultural note about modesty.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the words '俩' (two people) and '都' (both/all).

narration.subtitle_en: The father resolves the friendly back-and-forth. He says, [zh:哎，你们俩都聪明]. The word [zh:哎] is an interjection, like 'Hey' or 'Well'. The key words are [zh:俩] and [zh:都]. [zh:俩] is a colloquial word for 'two people', so [zh:你们俩] means 'you two'. [zh:都] means 'both' or 'all'. So the sentence means, 'Hey, you two are both smart'.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A colloquial term for '两个' (liǎng ge), meaning two people.
An adverb indicating that something applies to all members of a group mentioned.

segments.visual_notes: Show an animation grouping Wang Peng and Li You together, with the characters '俩' and '都' appearing above them.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to express an observation with '好像' and ask a '是不是' question.

narration.subtitle_en: A mother's sharp eyes notice a change. She says, [zh:你好像瘦了点儿], 'You seem to have lost a little weight'. [zh:好像] means 'to seem like', [zh:瘦] means 'thin', and [zh:点儿] means 'a little bit'. She then guesses the reason with a [zh:是不是] question: 'Is it that you were too busy with your part-time job...?' The word for part-time job is [zh:打工].

on_screen_text.focus_text: 小朋，你好像瘦了点儿。是不是打工太忙，没有时间吃饭？
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Xiǎo Péng, nǐ hǎoxiàng shòu le diǎnr. shì bu shì dǎ gōng tài máng, méiyǒu shíjiān chī fàn?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Xiao Peng, you seem to have lost some weight. Were you too busy with your part-time job to eat?

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used to express an impression or a guess.
The opposite of fat.
To do temporary work.
The indefinite continued progress of existence and events.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a thought bubble from the mother showing Wang Peng looking thinner. Highlight the words '好像' and '瘦'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to negate with '没' and make a comparison using '比'.

narration.subtitle_en: Wang Peng reassures his mom. He says, [zh:我没瘦], 'I haven't lost weight'. Notice the use of [zh:没] to negate a past action. He explains he often exercises, [zh:我常常运动]. Then he makes a comparison: [zh:身体比以前棒多了], 'My body is much stronger than before'. The structure is 'A [zh:比] B + Adjective'. Here, [zh:身体] is 'body', [zh:以前] is 'before', and [zh:棒] is a colloquial word for 'great' or 'strong'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我没瘦。我常常运动，身体比以前棒多了。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: wǒ méi shòu. wǒ chángcháng yùndòng, shēntǐ bǐ yǐqián bàng duō le.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I haven't lost weight. I exercise often, my body is much stronger than before.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Frequently; many times.
Activity requiring physical effort.
The physical structure of a person.
An informal adjective to express that something is excellent.

segments.visual_notes: Show Wang Peng flexing his arm. Animate the comparison structure 'A 比 B 棒'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to ask about someone's well-being using '怎么样'.

narration.subtitle_en: The father changes the topic to ask about another person, Xiao Hong. He asks, [zh:小红怎么样]. The phrase [zh:怎么样] is a versatile and extremely common way to ask 'How is...?' or 'What about...?'. You can use it to ask about people, things, or situations.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A question word used to ask about the condition or state of something or someone.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a question mark next to the father's head with '怎么样?' inside it.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn vocabulary for 'English level' and 'to improve'.

narration.subtitle_en: Wang Peng replies that Xiao Hong is doing well. He says, [zh:她很好], 'She is very good'. He then adds a specific detail about her progress: [zh:英文水平提高了很多]. Let's look at the new words. [zh:英文水平] means 'English level'. [zh:水平] literally means 'water level' and is used to mean 'level' or 'standard'. [zh:提高] means 'to improve' or 'to raise'. So, her English level has improved a lot.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 她很好，英文水平提高了很多。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: tā hěn hǎo, Yīngwén shuǐpíng tígāo le hěn duō.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: She's doing great, her English has improved a lot.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The language of England, now widely used in many parts of the world.
A position on a scale of amount, quantity, extent, or quality.
To make or become better.

segments.visual_notes: Show an icon for 'English' (e.g., a flag) with an upward arrow to represent '提高'.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand a sequence of suggestions and learn vocabulary for family and food.

narration.subtitle_en: The father suggests they leave the airport. He says [zh:走吧], 'Let's go'. He continues, [zh:我们上车以后，再慢慢儿地聊吧], 'We can chat slowly after we get in the car'. Finally, he reveals the exciting plan: [zh:爷爷、奶奶在烤鸭店等我们呢]. This introduces several new words: [zh:爷爷] is paternal grandfather, [zh:奶奶] is paternal grandmother, and [zh:烤鸭店] is a roast duck restaurant. Beijing is famous for its roast duck!

on_screen_text.focus_text: 走吧，我们上车以后，再慢慢儿地聊吧。爷爷、奶奶在烤鸭店等我们呢！
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: zǒu ba, wǒmen shàng chē yǐhòu, zài mànmānr de liáo ba. yéye, nǎinai zài kǎoyādiàn děng wǒmen ne!
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Let's go. We can chat slowly after we get in the car. Grandpa and Grandma are waiting for us at the roast duck restaurant!

highlight_words.explanation_en: To move from one place to another.
To board a vehicle.
At a slow speed.
The father of one's father.
The mother of one's father.
A restaurant that specializes in roast duck.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a car arriving, then transition to a picture of a delicious-looking roast duck.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand that repeating a key phrase can express interest or surprise.

narration.subtitle_en: Li You, who is likely not from Beijing, hears the plan and repeats the key phrase with interest: [zh:烤鸭店]. This simple repetition with a questioning tone is a very natural way to show you've heard something new and are curious or excited about it. It's a great way to keep a conversation going.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A restaurant that specializes in roast duck.

segments.visual_notes: Show a question mark and an exclamation point appearing over Li You's head.

segments.teaching_goal: To teach students how to use '还 (hái)' before a positive adjective to indicate something is 'okay' or 'acceptable', but not outstanding.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's look at our first grammar point: using [zh:还] before an adjective. In the dialogue, when his mom asks if he's exhausted, Wang Peng replies [zh:还好]. This literally means 'still good,' but it's used to say 'I'm okay' or 'It's acceptable.' This structure, [zh:还] plus a positive adjective, is very common for expressing that something meets a basic standard but isn't necessarily amazing. For example, you could say a restaurant is [zh:还不错], meaning 'pretty good,' or an apartment is [zh:还行], meaning 'not too bad.' It’s a great way to give a nuanced, modest opinion.

on_screen_text.notes: Expresses that something is acceptable, but not truly outstanding.

grammar_points.explanation_en: It's okay. / I'm okay. (From the dialogue)
That apartment is not too bad.

grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Nàge fànguǎn de cài hái búcuò.
grammar_points.explanation_en: That restaurant's food is pretty good.

segments.visual_notes: Animate the character '还' moving to combine with different adjectives like '好', '可以', '行', '不错'.

segments.teaching_goal: To introduce core Chinese kinship terms for the immediate paternal family, as seen in the dialogue.

narration.subtitle_en: Now let's dive into a big topic in Chinese culture: kinship terms. The system is more detailed than in English, often distinguishing between paternal and maternal sides. In our dialogue, we see several key terms. Wang Peng greets his parents as [zh:爸] and [zh:妈]. Later, his father mentions that [zh:爷爷] and [zh:奶奶] are waiting. These refer to the paternal grandfather and grandmother. Let's look at these basic terms for the father's side of the family.

segments.visual_notes: Display a simple family tree diagram showing the connection of these four roles to the speaker.

content.title: Core Paternal Family Terms
content.note: These terms are for the father's side of the family. Maternal relatives have different names.

segments.teaching_goal: To teach the cultural practice of using kinship terms like '叔叔' and '阿姨' to politely address elders who are not family.

narration.subtitle_en: Building on kinship terms, let's look at a very important cultural practice. When Li You meets Wang Peng's parents, she greets them with [zh:叔叔，阿姨，你们好]. [zh:叔叔] means 'uncle' and [zh:阿姨] means 'auntie'. But she's not related to them! In China, it's common and respectful for young people to address adults who are about their parents' age using these terms. It creates a sense of warmth and politeness. So, if you meet a friend's parents or even a helpful stranger of that generation, using [zh:叔叔] for a man and [zh:阿姨] for a woman is the perfect way to show respect.

on_screen_text.focus_text: Q: How to address a friend's parents? A: 叔叔 (for father) / 阿姨 (for mother)
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Q: How to address a friend's parents? A: 'Uncle' (for father) / 'Auntie' (for mother)
on_screen_text.notes: Use these respectful titles even for non-relatives.

grammar_points.explanation_en: A polite greeting for a friend's parents or other elders, using 'uncle' and 'auntie' as respectful titles.

segments.visual_notes: Show an animation of a younger person bowing slightly while greeting two older figures labeled '叔叔' and '阿姨'.

segments.teaching_goal: To review the key grammar and phrases from the lesson.

narration.subtitle_en: Great job! Let's quickly review what we've learned. We covered how to use [zh:还好] to say something is 'okay'. We also learned about the importance of specific kinship terms, like using [zh:叔叔] and [zh:阿姨] to politely address elders. Keep these key phrases in mind as you continue your studies.

highlight_words.pinyin: nǐ de Zhōngwén shuō de zhēn hǎo.

segments.visual_notes: Display the key phrases as a list on the screen, with each one appearing as it's mentioned.

Décomposons cela : [zh:北京] est Pékin, la capitale de la Chine. [zh:首都] signifie 'capitale', et [zh:机场] signifie 'aéroport'. Donc, les retrouvailles familiales ont lieu directement à l'aéroport.

highlight_words.explanation_en: La capitale de la Chine.
La ville principale d'un pays, qui est le siège du gouvernement.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: Composed of [zh:首] (shǒu), meaning 'head' or 'first', and [zh:都] (dū), meaning 'metropolis' or 'capital'. Together, they literally mean 'head city'.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Un endroit où les avions décollent et atterrissent régulièrement.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: Made of [zh:机] (jī), short for [zh:飞机] (fēijī) 'airplane', and [zh:场] (chǎng), meaning 'field' or 'large open space'. So, an 'airplane field'.

highlight_words.explanation_en: La capitale de la Chine.

segments.teaching_goal: Apprendre à demander si quelqu'un est épuisé en utilisant [zh:lèi huài le] 累坏了 et la particule de suggestion [zh:ba] 吧.

narration_track.subtitle_en: La mère exprime son inquiétude après un long vol. Elle demande : [zh:累坏了吧]. Décortiquons cela. [zh:累] signifie 'fatigué'. L'ajout de [zh:坏] après agit comme un complément, intensifiant le sens pour exprimer 'épuisé' ou 'terriblement fatigué'. La particule finale [zh:吧] adoucit la question, la transformant en une supposition délicate, comme on dirait 'Tu dois être épuisé, n'est-ce pas ?'.

content.focus_gloss_en: Tu dois être épuisé, n'est-ce pas ?

segments.teaching_goal: Apprendre à dire « Je vais bien » et à présenter un camarade de classe.

segments.teaching_goal: Apprendre à saluer respectueusement les parents d'un ami en utilisant « 叔叔 » [zh:shūshu] et « 阿姨 » [zh:āyí].

narration_track.subtitle_en: Li You salue très poliment les parents de Wang Peng. Elle dit : [zh:叔叔，阿姨，你们好]. Dans la culture chinoise, il est courant de s'adresser aux personnes de la génération de vos parents en utilisant [zh:叔叔] pour les hommes, ce qui signifie 'oncle', et [zh:阿姨] pour les femmes, ce qui signifie 'tante', même si elles ne sont pas de votre famille. C'est un signe de respect et de convivialité.

segments.teaching_goal: Apprendre à accueillir quelqu'un en utilisant '[zh:欢迎]'.

segments.teaching_goal: Apprendre la structure pour complimenter une action : Verbe + 得 + Adverbe + Adjectif.

narration_track.subtitle_en: La mère complimente Li You sur ses compétences linguistiques. Elle dit : [zh:你的中文说得真好], ce qui signifie 'Ton chinois est vraiment bien parlé'. Concentrons-nous sur la structure [zh:说得真好]. [zh:说] est le verbe 'parler'. Il est suivi de la particule [zh:得], qui relie le verbe à une description de la manière dont l'action est effectuée. [zh:真好] signifie 'vraiment bien'. Ce modèle 'verbe-得-description' est essentiel pour faire des compliments.

segments.teaching_goal: Apprendre à accepter modestement un compliment et à en attribuer le mérite à quelqu'un d'autre.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Li You répond au compliment avec modestie, une qualité très appréciée dans la culture chinoise. Elle dit d'abord [zh:谢谢], ce qui signifie 'Merci'. Ensuite, elle attribue le mérite à son ami : [zh:是因为王朋教得好], ce qui signifie 'C'est parce que Wang Peng a bien enseigné'. L'expression [zh:是因为] met l'accent sur la raison. Remarquez le même modèle à nouveau : [zh:教得好], qui signifie 'enseigne bien'.

segments.teaching_goal: Apprendre à utiliser '没' pour la négation et '比' pour faire une comparaison.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Wang Peng rassure sa mère. Il dit, [zh:我没瘦], 'Je n'ai pas maigri'. Remarquez l'utilisation de [zh:没] pour nier une action passée. Il explique qu'il fait souvent de l'exercice, [zh:我常常运动]. Ensuite, il fait une comparaison : [zh:身体比以前棒多了], 'Mon corps est bien plus fort qu'avant'. La structure est 'A [zh:比] B + Adjectif'. Ici, [zh:身体] signifie 'corps', [zh:以前] signifie 'avant', et [zh:棒] est un mot familier pour dire 'super' ou 'fort'.

segments.teaching_goal: Enseigner aux étudiants comment utiliser [zh:hái] avant un adjectif positif pour indiquer que quelque chose est « correct » ou « acceptable », mais pas exceptionnel.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Examinons notre premier point de grammaire : l'utilisation de [zh:还] devant un adjectif. Dans le dialogue, lorsque sa mère lui demande s'il est épuisé, Wang Peng répond [zh:还好]. Cela signifie littéralement « encore bien », mais c'est utilisé pour dire « Je vais bien » ou « C'est acceptable ». Cette structure, [zh:还] suivi d'un adjectif positif, est très courante pour exprimer que quelque chose répond à une norme de base, sans être nécessairement extraordinaire. Par exemple, on pourrait dire qu'un restaurant est [zh:还不错], ce qui signifie « plutôt bon », ou qu'un appartement est [zh:还行], ce qui signifie « pas mal du tout ». C'est une excellente façon de donner une opinion nuancée et modeste.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Plongeons maintenant dans un sujet important de la culture chinoise : les termes de parenté. Le système est plus détaillé qu'en français, faisant souvent la distinction entre la lignée paternelle et maternelle. Dans notre dialogue, nous voyons plusieurs termes clés. Wang Peng salue ses parents en les appelant [zh:爸] et [zh:妈]. Plus tard, son père mentionne que [zh:爷爷] et [zh:奶奶] attendent. Ces termes désignent le grand-père et la grand-mère paternels. Examinons ces termes de base pour la famille du côté du père.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Dans la continuité des termes de parenté, abordons une pratique culturelle très importante. Quand Li You rencontre les parents de Wang Peng, elle les salue avec [zh:叔叔，阿姨，你们好]. [zh:叔叔] signifie « oncle » et [zh:阿姨] signifie « tante ». Mais elle n'a aucun lien de parenté avec eux ! En Chine, il est courant et respectueux pour les jeunes de s'adresser aux adultes de l'âge de leurs parents en utilisant ces termes. Cela crée un sentiment de chaleur et de politesse. Ainsi, si vous rencontrez les parents d'un ami ou même un inconnu serviable de cette génération, utiliser [zh:叔叔] pour un homme et [zh:阿姨] pour une femme est la manière parfaite de montrer du respect.

1: Notre leçon commence par une description du cadre.
Le texte dit [zh:在北京首都机场], ce qui signifie 'À l'aéroport international de Pékin'.
Décomposons cela : [zh:北京] est Pékin, la capitale de la Chine.
Donc, les retrouvailles familiales ont lieu directement à l'aéroport.
2: Le père voit son fils et l'appelle par son nom, [zh:小朋].
C'est une manière courante et affectueuse d'attirer l'attention de quelqu'un.
'Xiao' est souvent utilisé comme préfixe pour les noms de personnes plus jeunes, ce qui le rend amical.
3: Wang Peng répond à son père en appelant ses deux parents : [zh:爸，妈].
[zh:爸] est le mot informel et le plus courant pour 'Papa', et [zh:妈] est pour 'Maman'.
Ce sont les premiers mots que la plupart des enfants chinois apprennent pour leurs parents.
4: La mère exprime son inquiétude après un long vol. Elle demande : [zh:累坏了吧].
L'ajout de [zh:坏] après agit comme un complément, intensifiant le sens pour exprimer 'épuisé' ou 'terriblement fatigué'.
La particule finale [zh:吧] adoucit la question, la transformant en une supposition délicate, comme on dirait 'Tu dois être épuisé, n'est-ce pas ?'.
5: Wang Peng répond qu'il va bien et présente son ami.
Il commence par [zh:还好], ce qui signifie 'Je vais bien' ou 'pas mal'.
Ensuite, il utilise l'expression clé pour les présentations : [zh:我给你们介绍一下], qui veut dire 'Permettez-moi de vous présenter'.
Enfin, il dit [zh:这是我的同学李友], ce qui se traduit par 'Voici mon camarade de classe, Li You'.
Le mot [zh:同学] signifie 'camarade de classe'.
6: Li You salue très poliment les parents de Wang Peng.

Dans la culture chinoise, il est courant de s'adresser aux personnes de la génération de vos parents en utilisant [zh:叔叔] pour les hommes, ce qui signifie 'oncle', et [zh:阿姨] pour les femmes, ce qui signifie 'tante', même si elles ne sont pas de votre famille.
C'est un signe de respect et de convivialité.
7: Le père répond chaleureusement à Li You.
Le mot clé ici est [zh:欢迎], qui signifie 'bienvenue'.
La phrase complète signifie 'Bienvenue à Pékin'.
C'est une expression très courante et hospitalière que vous entendrez souvent en arrivant dans un nouvel endroit en Chine.
8: La mère complimente Li You sur ses compétences linguistiques.
Elle dit : [zh:你的中文说得真好], ce qui signifie 'Ton chinois est vraiment bien parlé'.
Concentrons-nous sur la structure [zh:说得真好].
Il est suivi de la particule [zh:得], qui relie le verbe à une description de la manière dont l'action est effectuée.
Ce modèle 'verbe-得-description' est essentiel pour faire des compliments.
9: Li You répond au compliment avec modestie, une qualité très appréciée dans la culture chinoise.
Elle dit d'abord [zh:谢谢], ce qui signifie 'Merci'.
Ensuite, elle attribue le mérite à son ami : [zh:是因为王朋教得好], ce qui signifie 'C'est parce que Wang Peng a bien enseigné'.
L'expression [zh:是因为] met l'accent sur la raison.
Remarquez le même modèle à nouveau : [zh:教得好], qui signifie 'enseigne bien'.
10: Wang Peng fait également preuve de modestie.
Il dit [zh:哪里], qui signifie littéralement 'où ?', mais qui est utilisé ici comme une manière humble de dire 'Pas du tout' ou 'Vous me flattez' lorsqu'on reçoit un compliment.
Il renvoie ensuite le compliment à Li You en disant [zh:是因为你聪明], 'C'est parce que tu es intelligent'.
11: Le père met fin à cet échange amical.
Le mot [zh:哎] est une interjection, comme 'Hé' ou 'Eh bien'.
Les mots clés sont [zh:俩] et [zh:都].
[zh:俩] est un mot familier pour 'deux personnes', donc [zh:你们俩] signifie 'vous deux'.
Donc la phrase signifie : 'Hé, vous êtes tous les deux intelligents'.
12: L'œil aiguisé d'une mère remarque un changement.
Elle dit [zh:你好像瘦了点儿], 'Tu as l'air d'avoir un peu maigri'.
[zh:好像] signifie 'sembler', [zh:瘦] signifie 'mince', et [zh:点儿] signifie 'un petit peu'.
Elle devine ensuite la raison avec une question utilisant [zh:是不是] : 'Serait-ce que tu étais trop occupée avec ton petit boulot... ?'
Le mot pour 'petit boulot' est [zh:打工].
13: Il dit, [zh:我没瘦], 'Je n'ai pas maigri'.
Remarquez l'utilisation de [zh:没] pour nier une action passée.
Il explique qu'il fait souvent de l'exercice, [zh:我常常运动].
Ensuite, il fait une comparaison : [zh:身体比以前棒多了], 'Mon corps est bien plus fort qu'avant'.
Ici, [zh:身体] signifie 'corps', [zh:以前] signifie 'avant', et [zh:棒] est un mot familier pour dire 'super' ou 'fort'.
14: Le père change de sujet pour demander des nouvelles d'une autre personne, Xiao Hong.
L'expression [zh:怎么样] est une manière polyvalente et extrêmement courante de demander 'Comment va...?'
Vous pouvez l'utiliser pour poser des questions sur des personnes, des choses ou des situations.
15: Wang Peng répond que Xiao Hong va bien.
Il dit, [zh:她很好], 'Elle va très bien'.
Il ajoute ensuite un détail précis sur ses progrès : [zh:英文水平提高了很多].
[zh:水平] signifie littéralement 'niveau d'eau' et est utilisé pour désigner un 'niveau' ou un 'standard'.
Donc, son niveau d'anglais s'est beaucoup amélioré.

Il continue, [zh:我们上车以后，再慢慢儿地聊吧], « Nous pourrons discuter tranquillement une fois dans la voiture ».
Enfin, il révèle le plan passionnant : [zh:爷爷、奶奶在烤鸭店等我们呢].
Cela introduit plusieurs nouveaux mots : [zh:爷爷] est le grand-père paternel, [zh:奶奶] est la grand-mère paternelle, et [zh:烤鸭店] est un restaurant de canard laqué.
Pékin est célèbre pour son canard laqué !
